site: Synthetic example — public W3C WAI Before-and-After demo (inaccessible page)
note: A minimal, well-formed example plan (per reference/plan-schema.md) against W3C's intentionally-inaccessible Before-and-After demo. It shows the two finding shapes: an attribute/CSS fix that compiles to a Stitch, and a content-judgement finding left as a developer recommendation. Use it as a template for your own plans.

reviewer.name: the reviewer
reviewer.environment: Windows 11, Chrome, 200%/400% zoom, keyboard-only, screen reader

findings.subject: No working skip link to bypass the repeated navigation
findings.explanation: A keyboard or screen-reader user must tab through the whole navigation on every page; there is no mechanism to jump straight to the main content.
findings.recommendation: Add a visually-hidden skip link as the first focusable element, targeting the main content region, and give that region tabindex=-1 so focus can land on it.

findings.subject: Page language is not set programmatically
findings.explanation: The document has no lang attribute, so assistive technology can't pick the correct pronunciation rules.
findings.recommendation: Set the document language on the html element.

findings.subject: Informative images need accurate text alternatives
findings.explanation: Several images convey information but lack a meaningful alt attribute. Whether a given alternative is correct is a content judgement, so this is recorded as a developer recommendation rather than auto-patched.
findings.recommendation: Author an alt attribute for each informative image that conveys the same information as the image; mark purely decorative images with an empty alt.
